llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Studies geometric behavior of plane curves under the Square Root Velocity (SRV) transform, deriving explicit formulas for transformed speed/curvature and identifying invariants (e.g., total curvature/turning number). Extends elastic SRV-based comparison to manifold-valued and augmented curves that generate constrained surface classes (tubes, ruled surfaces, spherical strips) and to hurricane tracks augmented with intensity, enabling intrinsic geodesic computation in corresponding shape spaces.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Riemannian elastic shape analysis of immersed curves with reparametrization/isometry invariance. Uses SRV transform for Euclidean curves and a homogeneous-space SRV extension via horizontal lifts in Lie groups H with M=H/K, mapping curves to H×L2(D,𝔥) to compute distances/geodesics by minimizing over K. For plane curves, applies Frenet equations to derive SRV-transformed speed and curvature relations; for surfaces, represents each constrained surface class by an augmented generating curve (e.g., (γ,r) in ℝ4 for tubes; (γ,v) in ℝ3×S2 for ruled; (γ,r) on S2×ℝ for spherical strips via exp map) and compares via curve metrics. Geodesics computed with Riemannian optimization implemented in Morphomatics.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Demonstrative examples/visualizations of shortest (geodesic) deformation paths for: tubes and ruled surfaces in ℝ3 (represented as curves in ℝ4 or augmented spaces), spherical strips on S2 with bandwidth function, and Atlantic hurricane tracks from NOAA HURDAT2 (lat/long every 6h) represented as discrete trajectories on S2 with auxiliary maxwind intensity (S2×ℝ). Uses the SRV/homogeneous-space distance and Riemannian optimization to compute geodesic paths; no quantitative benchmark metrics reported.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Primarily theoretical and illustrative; lacks systematic quantitative evaluation, benchmarks, or statistical validation. SRV framework requires immersed curves; transformed curve may fail to be immersion when curvature and speed-derivative share zeros. Homogeneous-space SRV relies on choice of horizontal lifts and minimization over isotropy K; computational aspects and convergence guarantees not detailed. Surface analysis limited to constrained classes uniquely determined by generating augmented curves; may not generalize to arbitrary surfaces. Hurricane data treated as discrete trajectories; discretization/noise and temporal sampling effects not analyzed.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Apply framework to broader real-world longitudinal datasets and perform full statistical analyses on shape spaces (means, hypothesis tests, PGA). Develop hierarchical/group-wise trend models for augmented manifold-valued trajectories; extend to classification/prediction of events (e.g., hurricanes) using geodesic features. Improve computational methods (scalable optimization, discretization schemes, robustness to noise/holonomy bias) and extend constructions to additional constrained surface families or more general surface representations.

research_study_list.title: Compositional Semantic Parsing with Large Language Models
research_study_list.full_text: A ‘Human-in-the-Loop’ approach for Information Extraction from Privacy Policies under Data Scarcity Michael Gebauer, Faraz Maschhur, Nicola Leschke, Elias Gr ¨unewald and Frank Pallas Information Systems Engineering - TU Berlin Berlin Email: {mg, f.maschhur, nl, eg, fp }@ise.tu-berlin.de Preprint (2023-05-22) before final copy-editing of an accepted peer-reviewed paper to appear in the 2023 IEEE European Symposium on Security and Privacy Workshops (EuroS&PW). The Version of Record can be found here: https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/xpl/conhome/1820965/all-proceedings Abstract—Machine-readable representations of privacy poli- cies are door openers for a broad variety of novel privacy- enhancing and, in particular, transparency-enhancing tech- nologies (TETs). In order to generate such representations, transparency information needs to be extracted from writ- ten privacy policies. However, respective manual annotation and extraction processes are laborious and require expert knowledge. Approaches for fully automated annotation, in turn, have so far not succeeded due to overly high error rates in the specific domain of privacy policies. In the end, a lack of properly annotated privacy policies and respective machine-readable representations persists and enduringly hinders the development and establishment of novel technical approaches fostering policy perception and data subject informedness. In this work, we present a prototype system for a ‘Human-in-the-Loop’approach to privacy policy annotation that integrates ML-generated suggestions and ultimately human annotation decisions. We propose an ML-based sug- gestion system specifically tailored to the constraint of data scarcity prevalent in the domain of privacy policy annotation. On this basis, we provide meaningful predictions to users thereby streamlining the annotation process. Additionally, we also evaluate our approach through a prototypical imple- mentation to show that our ML-based extraction approach provides superior performance over other recently used extraction models for legal documents. 1. Introduction Machine-readable, structured representations of pri- vacy policies are a door-opener for a broad variety of user-facing privacy-enhancing technologies, ranging from privacy icons [1, 2] over privacy dashboards [3] to in- teractive privacy chatbots [4]. Especially when it comes to technically mediated approaches for overcoming the inherent shortcomings of written, legalese privacy policies in matters of comprehensible transparency information (as, e.g., mandated in Art. 12 ff. of the GDPR), the to- be-presented information must be available in a machine- readable and automatically processable form [5–7]. In ad- dition, this machine-readable form must comprise relevant information at a sufficient level of detail and preciseness to actually allow for novel presentation approaches and to preserve legal meaningfulness. Formal, machine-readable representation languages (e.g., [5, 7, 8]) play an important part here, but still leave open the gap of transforming pre-existing, textual privacy policies into respective machine-readable representations. As long as data controllers cannot be forced to provide machine-readable privacy policies themselves, performing this transformation is thus one of the most urgent obstacles for alternative – and, from the perspective of data subjects, often preferable – policy presentations to actually gain momentum. Therefore, we propose a ‘Human-in-the-Loop’ pri- vacy policy annotation system which facilitates the tran- sition from purely textual privacy policies to machine- readable ones. We present an architecture as well as a fully functional prototypical implementation of such a system that enables privacy researchers to continuously annotate privacy policies with required efforts being sig- nificantly reduced through suggestions generated with machine learning (ML) and with employed ML models continuously learning new annotations. To provide high suggestion quality from the very beginning, we employ machine learning algorithms specifically tailored to the additional constraint of data scarcity. To demonstrate the capabilities and the viability of our approach as well as to assess the suitability of dif- ferent classification approaches and language models for the domain of privacy policies, we furthermore present a benchmark on a consciously small dataset to simulate the situation of data scarcity. Throughout our benchmark, we will focus solely on data subject rights, as they are stated in full sentences and do not call for pointy extraction of highly specific facts often codified in single words, which would have been exaggerated for a first evaluation of our overall approach. Given this sentence-focused extraction, the complexity of the task remains, as data subject rights share a lot of semantic similarities with each other. Here, we decided to use Sentence Bert (SBERT) , as it enables a significantly more meaningful representation of sentences than other approaches [9] and thus is more suited for sentence-based information extraction. Our contributions therefore are: • A novel, so far not pursued approach to semi- automated privacy policy annotation, combining manual expert annotations of privacy policies with ML-generated suggestions in a continuous feed- back loop • A fully functional and easy-to-use information ex- traction system, providing an annotation interface for users with a continuous learning component and ML-based suggestions for manual annotation arXiv:2305.15006v2  [cs.CY]  31 May 2023• Benchmarks covering three established informa- tion extraction algorithms on a public dataset to test for their capabilities under data scarcity in the specific domain of privacy policies Our respective considerations and findings evolve as follows: In section 2 we give an overview of already existing work in the field and outline the uniqueness of our approach. Section 3 introduces the basic concepts of our ML-based suggestion engine as well as its model candidates. The implementation of our general approach in the form of a concrete system is presented in section 4, whereas section 5 presents an evaluation for all model candidates and outlines the capabilities of our systems. Section 6 concludes. 2. Background & Related Work Automated policy information extraction employing modern methods of natural language processing (NLP) or understanding (NLU) has been a major line of research over the past years [10–14]. A well-known framework for automated privacy policy information extraction is Polisis [14]. Polisis aims to facilitate natural language queries on real-world privacy policies. For this purpose, privacy policies are scraped from the web and divided into segments, which are then classified according to a privacy taxonomy proposed by [12] in a machine learning layer. The classification results are then matched with the classes inferred from the natural language query. The machine learning layer used in Polisis comprises privacy- specific static word embeddings, which are then used to classify the segments in a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)[14]. Different from Polisis, however, we do not aim for identifying coarse-grained sections of written privacy poli- cies broadly dedicated to different, rather general subject areas or for respective “reading hints”. Instead, we want to extract fine-granular and precise transparency infor- mation and to thereby transform written privacy policies into semantically rich and analyzable, machine-readable representations. The challenges to be solved and the tech- nical approaches to be considered viable for this purpose strongly differ from those known and used in the Polisis context. Given the highly domain-specific, legalese language and the complex verbal constructs to be found in privacy policies, [5], fully automated retrieval of privacy policy information for machine-readable formats appears unsuit- able as respective results are still severely limited in mat- ters of capability and reliability [12, 14]. To a large extent, this limitation can be ascribed to the scarcity of labeled training data – a common and well-known problem in automated privacy policy annotation: Given that training datasets have to be laboriously handcrafted and checked by legal experts, automated policy information extraction is hindered by tremendous efforts in matters of time and cost [11, 12, 15, 16]. An alternative approach lies in consciously disregard- ing the goal of completely reliable and accurate policy information extraction in favor of mere “considerable hints” pointing towards sections of a privacy policy pre- sumably denoted to a certain subject or a certain category of information to be provided [10, 12, 13, 17]. How- ever, those approaches rather support – like the Polisis approach mentioned above – navigability within written privacy policies and are not eligible for creating detailed and accurate machine-readable representations of privacy policies. Additionally, most of these rely on a benchmark dataset introduced by [12], which proposes a privacy taxonomy with ten top level categories that are not aligned with a specific privacy regulation and therefore lack le- gal expressiveness desirable for such a machine-readable representation. Also, such benchmark datasets may cause foreseeable problems such as distributional shifts resulting from changes in privacy policies or well-known limitations when applying benchmark datasets to the real-world [18, 19]. Therefore, we consciously abstain from these ap- proaches. Instead, we employ a standardized corpus of machine-readable privacy policies together with their cor- responding textual basis, in an information extraction system. This system allows us to train and extract concrete legal statements, throughout an once-in-a-lifetime training phase. Regarding machine-readable representation of privacy policy information, a lack of appropriate formats and standardization for representing information in a unified form has previously been identified [10]. Consequently, standardized languages for representing privacy policy information in machine-readable form are indispensable here. Early such languages include P3P [20, 21] and PPL [22]. However, these are not aligned to a specific privacy regulation and therefore do not include all legally manda- tory information. To overcome this problem, GDPR- aligned and semantically rich transparency languages have recently been introduced [7, 8] and can serve as means for standardizing privacy policy information representation. Given its well-crafted expressiveness and the comprehen- sive programming toolkit available, we decided to use TILT [7] and the corresponding set of machine-readable policies1 as our annotated training set. 3. Approach: Guided Information Retrieval In light of the above-mentioned considerations, we herein propose an easy-to-use privacy policy information extraction system that supports human annotators with ML-based annotation suggestions, as depicted in figure 1. Annotators’ ultimate decisions are then fed back into continuously (re)training the machine learning model. Through the conscious combination of ML-based sugges- tions, ultimately human annotation, and a feedback loop to continuously improve suggestion quality, we preserve the preciseness of human privacy policy annotation while continuously decreasing annotation efforts under the con- ditions of data scarcity we face in the domain of privacy policy information retrieval. Within this paper, we will focus on the extraction of data subject rights, as they serve as a perfect first example for semi-automated privacy policy information retrieval: On the one hand, data subject rights are typically provided in the form of complete sentences, making the automated 1. https://github.com/Transparency-Information-Language/tilt-corpus███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ Textual privacy policies { … } Manual annotation ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ Structured,  machine-readable transparency information ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ML-supported annotation Automated  mapping of  annotations Training Privacy Icons Chatbot Browser extension Dashboard Disclosure analysis … TILTer TILTify ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ ███████████ ██████████ ██████████ Figure 1: General approach extraction less resource-intensive than, for instance, ex- tracting multiple distinct purposes on a word level. On the other hand, different data subject rights are formulated in quite similar form in a given privacy policy, making them non-trivial to correctly extract and distinguish. The respective data subject rights covered herein are given in table 1. References Consumer Right 13(2c) 14(2d) Right to Withdraw Consent 13(2b) 14(2c) 15(1e) Right to Data Portability 13(2b) 14(2c) 15(1e) Right to Correction or Deletion 13(2d) 14(2e) 15(1f) Right to Complaint 13(2b) 14(2c) 15(1) Right to Information TABLE 1: Data subject rights and their respective refer- ence in the GDPR For the approach, we assume that a privacy policy (B) always contains a data subject right c, as we want to abstain from completion checks or other approaches (as in, e.g., [23, 24]). As data subject rights might be individual sentences in a paragraph, we simplify the approach by retrieving the whole paragraph. Hence, finding the correct paragraph that contains a data subject right c is the core problem of our Guided Information Retrieval approach and is achieved by recommending a set of candidate para- graphs S – that might contain the respective data subject right – to the user. This also introduces data imbalance, as within one document only a few sentences might contain the respective data subject right. Besides data scarcity this makes c hard to retrieve as well. 3.1. Problem Definition Formalizing the problem further, a privacy policy B can be divided into its respective paragraphs, which we call blobs bi. As policy sections containing data subject rights can span multiple sentences, we use these blobs for our information retrieval process. Thus, when trying to extract the data subject right c, we always try to retrieve a set of candidate blobs S. In case a blob contains a data subject right, we refer to it as ci = 1 and call it an annotation. In turn, if it does not, it will be ci = 0 . Note that this is different from S as c refers to a true data subject right, whereas S is a set of potential candidates for the data subject right in question. Hence, we can formalize our approach as part of a classification problem. S = {bi : max ∀bi∈B P (ci = 1|bi, θ)} Thus, we assume that there exists a perfect retrieval distribution P (ci = 1 |bi, θ), with true parameter θ that enables an exact retrieval of a data subject right. In a perfect case, S would thus always contain blobs that actually contain the to-be-annotated data subject right, meaning that S = {bi : ci = 1 ∀i ∈ B}. The goal for our ML model, in turn, is to find the perfect retrieval distribution P (ci = 1 |bi, θ) via an estimator ˆf(bi, ˆθ), which we will call extraction model. Under data scarcity , this estimator ˆf, trained on a historical dataset, will suffer from poor estimation perfor- mance and inevitably make wrong predictions as probabil- ities are not calibrated well enough. Therefore, we opted for a guided approach, where we rely on user feedback and ultimately human decisions while continuously improving model performance by updating the parameters ˆθ on the basis of these decisions. The feedback provided by users are marked blobs, which are stating whether these partic- ular blobs actually contain a given data subject right or not. These markings are simply called annotations and are generated by users through a dedicated, function-rich an- notation interface integrating ML-generated suggestions, full-text search, etc. In case an annotator marks a specific data subject right, this translates to ci = 1 for a particular blob bi. This way, users continuously generate new annotations that are used for training the estimator and thereby refine retrieval capacities of ˆf for data subjects rights. New annotations for privacy policies other than our policy of concern B are depicted by ∆b. However, as ˆf might not be suited to approximate P (ci = 1 |bi, θ) well enough, and we also have to provide users with feedback from the model through recommendations they are reviewing, we have to refine our information retrieval problem to present the highest k predictions from ˆf to the user. Hence, our problem is refined to: S = {bi : ˆf(bi, ˆθ(∆b)) ≥ ν ∀bi ∈ B}, (1) where ν is the minimum of the highest k entries in ˆf(bi, ˆθ(∆b)). As mentioned above, in order to facilitate the feedback loop we will pass on our recommendations S to users, so that they can generate new annotations for feedback ∆b more quickly. With this in mind, it becomes obvious that meaningful probability estimates of ˆf are vital for a well-functioning feedback loop. We emphasize that the k-based retrieval of blobs within our approach generalizes to many other information retrieval problems. 3.2. Extraction Model Options The problem of finding an ˆf that appropriately cap- tures P (ci = 1 |bi, θ) becomes particularly challenging under data scarcity. Therefore, we decided to implement and investigate multiple model candidates for ˆf. Static Word Embeddings & Naive Bayes. Static word embeddings have a long history, starting with One-Hot encoded vectors, TF-IDF [25] and also Latent SemanticFigure 2: System Architecture of TILTer & TILTify, which form the Information Extraction System Analysis [26]. Deep Learning based Embeddings first used neural networks to create meaningful vector representa- tions of words, such as Word2Vec [27]. Spacy offers one of the so-called static word embedding techniques, namely Token2Vec2. Token2Vec employs a Convolutional Neural Network, together with Hash Embedding of sub-features of individual words. These embeddings suffer from a lack of context awareness, as static embeddings are context- independent and do not account for the context a singular word appears in [28]. For our purposes, we use these embeddings and pass them on to a Gaussian Naive Bayes Classifier. Herein, every dimension of the embedding is assumed to follow a gaussian distribution. These dimen- sions are assumed to be independent of each other, as a Gaussian Naive Bayes Classifier links all dimensions solely to the target variable. The target variable in our case is a label on whether a specific blob contains a specific data subject right or not. The static embeddings produced by the above-stated model represent singular blobs within the privacy policy. BinaryBERT. BERT [29] is a transformer-based language model, which is capable of solving multiple NLP tasks. It is divided into two major steps: pre-training and fine- tuning. The pre-training involves a Masked Language Model approach, where 15 percent of all words are omit- ted and then predicted based on their context, and Next Sentence Prediction, where the model has to identify sentences that are following after another, from random ones. After this pre-training phase, BERT can be fine- tuned for specific tasks, like Named-Entity Recognition, Question Answering, Sequence Tagging and many more. As BERT showed superior performance over many NLP tasks, also for text classification [29, 30] and showed promising performance in other privacy policy extraction approaches [17], we decided to utilize it as well. For our purposes, we decided to employ the implementation of huggingface, which provides a BERT model specifically for sequence classification 3. We also use a pre-trained BERT model, which we fine-tune on our data subject rights identification task. We pass blobs to the BERT- model and provide training labels, which indicate whether a blob makes a statement about a specific data subject right or not. One BERT model is only suited for one particular data subject right, making it a binary classification task. 2. https://spacy.io/api/tok2vec 3. https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/model doc/bert# transformers.BertForSequenceClassification We therefor call this mode candidateBinary Bert (BBERT) and use Binary Cross Entropy as a loss function for opti- mization. As an optimizer, we use Adam with Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization [31]. SentenceBERT. SBERT is an addition to BERT. As BERT is not well suited for sentence classification or information retrieval [9], SBERT tries to overcome this by utilizing a siamese network and a collection of losses. These losses aim to tune sentence embeddings based on BERT for a stronger semantic distinction, which arises from the supplied training corpus. In our approach, we utilized the Triplet Loss Function, as in equation 2 [9]. J(q, bp, bn) = max(||q − bi||2 − ||q − bj||2 + ϵ, 0), (2) where i contains the specific data subject right and j does not. For our purposes, we set q to the legal texts about data subject rights stated in the GDPR articles in table 1 and matched these to the data subject right mentioned in the privacy policies. This had two major advantages over the other two approaches and the other loss options provided in SBERT. Firstly, we could increase the size of the training data respectively, as we had to pass all possible combinations of fitting and unfitting blobs into the model and secondly, by ϵ we had direct control over the distinction between fitting or unfitting blobs within a policy. Additionally, this approach also reduces the sever- ity of data imbalance due to the Triplet Loss Function. 4. Annotation System The information extraction system is divided into two major parts – TILTer4 and TILTify5 – and represents the prototypical implementation of our Guided Information Retrieval approach. Of these, the former one is responsible for communication with the user through an annotation interface and the persistence of users’ annotations. The latter is the learning and inference module, which trains on persisted user annotations and provides suggestions when processing respective textual privacy policies. In figure 2, an overview of the whole system architecture is presented. In particular, we receive user inputs via the annotation interface, through which human annotators can navigate to different annotation tasks and annotate the respective 4. https://github.com/DaSKITA/tilter 5. https://github.com/DaSKITA/tiltifytransparency information labels (TILT labels). These an- notations are then processed in a backend, powered by a Python Flask app, and saved in a MongoDB. Depending on the users’ inputs, the TILTer sends content to different endpoints of TILTify, which either trig- ger a training or inference procedure within TILTify. All content received in TILTify goes through a parser first, as we send privacy policies with their respective annotations to TILTify as JSON strings. The policy parser converts them into a Document object, which is used within TILTify for internal communication between components. Infer- ence and training within TILTify is orchestrated through the Extraction Manager that is responsible for triggering routines within the respective Extractors. When starting the system, initially all Extractors are trained on a small dataset, which provides some annotations for data subject rights. Outputs received from every Extractor are ulti- mately propagated back to the TILTer and then presented to the user as considerable hints. This loop can then be continuously repeated and even executed in parallel by multiple human-in-the-loop annotators for guided infor- mation retrieval. 4.1. Annotating Privacy Policies All privacy statements to be annotated are fed into TILTer through API calls, saved into the application database and exposed to users through the Annotation Interface. Subsequent user interaction is conducted di- rectly in the Annotation Interface as well. Annotations applied in there pass through the TILTer backend, are saved and forwarded to TILTify, where they may invoke training processes. The candidates offered for annotation are specified in a schema file in TILTer. This schema file enables TILTer to be used for the annotation of an entire TILT document. Each candidate offered for annotation is thus an element in a TILT-document, formerly introduced as TILT-labels. Figure 3: TILTer Annotation Interface Annotation Interface. Figure 3 shows the Annotation Interface, which enables well-informed annotation of pri- vacy policies using a pre-defined set of TILT-labels ob- tained from the schema file 6. Predictions obtained from models provided by TILTify are offered to support the user and thus are the formerly defined set of suggestions S. All TILT-labels offered for the annotation are explained in more detail under the retractable Label Description table. A second retractable table encloses the TILTify predictions, through which we enable easy navigation to the marked blob. Below the tables on the left, the available TILT-labels are shown while and on the right, current an- notations are displayed. The policy text is presented below these TILT-labels. Annotations are made by selecting a label and marking the passage to be annotated. The TILT- label overview continues to be shown when scrolling. The annotation functionalities of the Annotation Interface are based on Label Studio Frontend 7. Task Creation. The TILTer backend allows for the pro- cessing of privacy policies through REST API calls, which create a task containing the policy text as well as a set of TILT-labels. Since TILT labels are ordered hierarchically, annotating certain blobs leads to the creation of new tasks on the same policy, with TILT-labels from the next hierarchical level. This way, we ensure the user is not overwhelmed by the numerous annotation options through numerous TILT-labels. 4.2. Learning & Inference As previously stated, TILTify is responsible for learn- ing and inference within our system. It either generates annotation suggestions S for users or uses their inputs ∆b to improve the model performance. The entire communi- cation within TILTify happens in the form of a Document class, which represents the respective data and follows the notation we introduced in section 3.1 for blobs and documents. We designed TILTify to be extensible for other types of documents as well, while making it compatible to changing legal requirements. Policy Parser. The policy parser is responsible for con- verting a privacy policy provided in the form of a JSON string into the Document class. A Document class wraps a list of blobs, which contain paragraphs within a privacy policy, thus representing bi ∈ B. A blob contains the text of a paragraph and annotation objects, that are either provided by users or predicted by models, similar to the annotation we introduced earlier ci. So far, we do not provide a token-based annotation class, as we only focus on data subject rights . However, we are aware that TILT will at a later stage also require annotations on a token level as well (e.g., controller name, third-country transfers, etc.). Extractor. The Extractor provides an object for standard- ized communication between the Extraction Manager and the extraction routines and models ˆf. Thus, an Extrac- tor maps a provided TILT-label to an extraction model. 6. This schema file follows the available building blocks of the TILT schema. 7. https://github.com/heartexlabs/label-studio-frontendThe label represents the content to be extracted from the Document object, which is a subset of all annota- tion objects within a Document object, that matches the label, as there are more than one annotation within a Document. As described above, we only provide sentence- based annotations so far, hence Extractors also focus on sentence-based information retrieval. Annotations within a Document object are either inferred or used for train- ing by extraction models. Therefore, extraction models wrap models of an existing ML library (e.g., huggingface, scitkit-learn, pytorch), to define their prediction, training, loading, and saving routines, as well as tying them to a data preprocessor. Data preprocessors are creating the data format the model needs for inference and training out of the Document objects provided together with their annotations. When a prediction is performed by an extrac- tion model, writing predicted content into the respective Document object is done by the Extractor as well and is then propagated to the extraction manager. Extraction Manager. The Extraction Manager orches- trates Extractors, depending on the inputs received over the REST API. The extraction manager contains a model- and an extractor registry, where it contains all possible extraction models (see 3.2) and all existing Extractors that are created out of the existing extraction models and a pro- vided label. Orchestration is done via interaction with the extractor registry within the extraction manager, making it thus the central point of access for training, prediction, loading, saving, and initialization of all Extractors. It also passes on the results from Extractors to the REST API, so that they can be communicated to TILTer. 5. Evaluation In order to prove the validity of our approach, we conducted multiple experiments. We performed informa- tion retrieval tasks for data subject rights over a formerly annotated dataset and checked for the validity of model estimates to evaluate the actual applicability of the differ- ent extraction model options under data scarcity. Dataset. We created a public dataset 8, consisting of 60 annotated policies according to the TILT format in Ger- man language. Each of these contains differing amounts of data subject rights, given real-world privacy policies’ significant variance in this regard. The dataset can be used by other research to measure against our benchmarks. Again resembling real-world givens, the data exhibits a reasonable imbalance: in total, there are 16635 blobs, whereas they only contain a few data subject rights. Table 3 illustrates the number of blobs for every data subject right in the last column. Some data subject rights have multiple annotations within one document. In such cases, we determine the information retrieval to be successful as soon as one of the entries is retrieved by ˆf. Data scarcity manifests in the fact that we only used 60 documents for training and testing. The dataset was created through iterative cycles of annotation rounds. In the first round, student researchers 8. https://github.com/DaSKITA/tiltify/tree/main/data/annotated policies annotated the data, followed by a validation performed in the second round by more senior members of the project team. Flaws in annotations in the first round could thus be removed, ensuring more validity in provided annotations. Experimental Setting. For all models, we decided to use the same training and test data. Each of the models was trained for two repetitions over that same split, to account for different model initializations of parameters. Thus we are ruling out as many random effects as possible to main- tain adequate comparability. Hence we always report mean values for classification metrics and the respective stan- dard deviation. Training epochs for the Gaussian Naive Bayes model were set to 100, while SBERT and BBERT only use 5 training epochs, as they are fine-tuned for the task of data subject right retrieval. For all models, we use the default hyperparameter values, except for BBERT as we had to define an optimizer, where we choose Adam, with Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization [31] with learning rate 1 · 10−3 and weight decay of 1 · 10−5. As the dataset has a high data imbalance, we trained all models with a weighted sampling approach, where training examples were down- and upsampled to achieve an equal amount for paragraphs with ( ci = 1) and without a data subject right ( ci = 0). As we use German privacy policies for our experiment, we used a German version of BERT9 to obtain meaningful representations in BBERT and SBERT. k-Rank Metrics & Classification. The k-rank metrics describe the highest k model outputs, whereas an infor- mation retrieval with this approach was considered to be successful if among the k outputs of ˆf a real data subject right ci = 1 can be found (thus, when the correct paragraph was among those suggested to an annotator). The resulting F1-Scores in table 3 are clearly in favor of SBERT, as it outperforms the other two models in all metrics for all data subject rights. The surprisingly strong performance of the model might be attributed to its better- suited sentence embeddings and stronger capabilities of manipulating them, due to the Triplet Loss Function. It is also obvious that some data subject rights are harder to classify than others. The Right to Data Portability and the Right to Deletion seem to be quite hard to identify for some models. Despite being more complex, BBERT does not perform significantly better than the Gaussian Naive Bayes model. This might be attributed to the data- hungry nature of BERT and the tendency of non-deep learning models to perform good also on fewer data. Nev- ertheless, the static word embeddings used in Gaussian Naive Bayes might contribute to its poor performance, as the embeddings might not be able to account for the 9. https://huggingface.co/dbmdz/bert-base-german-casedBinaryBERT SentenceBERT GaussianNB #Blobs Right to Information 0.00 (0.00) 0.93 (0.05) 0.18 (0.08) 83 Right to Deletion 0.00 (0.00) 0.86 (0.05) 0.00 (0.00) 87 Right to Data Portability 0.00 (0.00) 0.86 (0.05) 0.00 (0.00) 77 Right to Withdraw Consent 0.08 (0.12) 0.90 (0.00) 0.00 (0.00) 95 Right to Complain 0.15 (0.00) 0.93 (0.03) 0.29 (0.00) 80 TABLE 3: 5-Rank F1-Scores for all models over all data subject rights. complexity of the task. Both Gaussian Naive Bayes and BBERT seem to strongly suffer from data scarcity, as they do not provide any meaningful extractions. For this case, it remains obvious that SBERT is the candidate of choice when confronted with data scarcity as prevalent in the domain of privacy policy information extraction. In table 2, the k-rank metrics also underline that SBERT is way better at making suggestions than the other two models: Using the example of the Right to Withdraw Consent, the first k suggestions of SBERT actually contain a fitting paragraph way more often than those provided by the others. Even when increasing the number k of estimated paragraphs to consider, the other two models are not able to catch up. However, looking at the classification performance of all models, SBERT does not seem to be remarkably better. Hence, it might not be superior in the exact identification of data subject rights, but rather seems to give better probability estimates for data, which are more useful in our human-in-the-loop approach. Calibration. Stronger semantic expressiveness for sen- tences also becomes obvious when we look at the cali- bration of the models. For this purpose, we will make use of the Brier-Score [32], which is defined as BS = 1 N NX i=1 (ci − ˆf(bi, ˆθ))2 (3) and calculates the squared error between the model estimates and the real label ( ci), given a blob bi, over all existing blobs in the dataset N. Hence, the better calibrated a model, the lower is the Brier Score. A well- calibrated model gives more-aligned probability estimates, as it is more proficient in expressing uncertainty about its predictions due to its estimates. Thus well-calibrated models should be more proficient in a k-ranking approach. In table 4 we can see the Brier scores for our respective models for every data subject right. Herein we choose to adjust the Brier Score with sample weights so that the data imbalance mentioned earlier is represented in the score and adjusted for the few occurrences of data subject rights. Throughout all data subject rights, SBERT has far lower Brier Scores, which indicate a better calibration than the other models. This shows that the semantics of the embeddings created by SBERT seem to express data subject rights way better, which is in line with the findings of [9]. Hence, despite data scarcity, SBERT gives well-adjusted semantic representations, which makes it far more fitting for information retrieval under data scarcity than other models. However, BBERT still seems to be better calibrated than the Gaussian Naive Bayes. Still, both are rather incapable of successfully extracting data subject rights. Conclusion In this paper, we propose a ML-assisted Guided In- formation Retrieval approach to streamline the process of privacy policy annotation. We design and prototypically implement a respective Annotation System which facili- tates continuous privacy policy annotation in a ” human- in-the-loop” manner supported by ML-generated sugges- tions. Using the example of data subject rights, we showed that using SBERT to generate respective suggestions is far superior to any other established models for the extraction of information out of privacy policies, particularly in k- based retrieval scenarios like the one employed for our Guided Information Retrieval approach. Insofar, it is sur- prising that SBERT has so far not broadly been used in the field of (semi-) automated privacy policy annotation. Our prototypical Annotation System can of course be improved further, especially by introducing a more production-ready TILTify architecture with technologies like Apache Spark10, ML-Flow11, or other MLOps Frame- works, which can take the serving of the Extractors to the next level and thus open it for more users. Additionally, relevant legal information to be extracted from privacy policies is not limited to sentences or paragraphs but also includes single words (tokens), which might be worth retrieving. Thus, investigating the predictive power and validity of our approach for word-based extraction is one of the next steps to take. We are also aware, that the retrieval of highly specific facts on a word level, may call for more complex and adjusted models, making model choice dependent on the legal facts one wants to extract. Lastly, continuous re-training of the models based on human annotations should successively increase suggestion quality further. However, faulty annotations by human error, may also introduce bias into trained models. Thus continuous testing of models on a provided test dataset maybe required, while replacement of a new model version should be guided by performance measures on the respective test dataset. As a first prototype, however, our system already proves our point that Guided Information Retrieval via SBERT can significantly accelerate the extraction of legal information from privacy policies. Further research in this 10. https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/ 11. https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/index.htmldirection should – at least in the medium term – pave the way for an easier and economically more feasible transfer of written, legalese privacy policies into machine- readable representations. In the end, this will also spur and accelerate the development of transparency-enhancing technologies for presenting legally relevant information to data subjects in novel, technically mediated, and more comprehensible ways. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Addresses the problem that fully automated extraction of fine-grained, legally meaningful transparency information from privacy policies fails under high error rates and severe labeled-data scarcity. Proposes a human-in-the-loop (HITL) annotation workflow that combines expert decisions with ML-generated paragraph-level suggestions and continuous retraining to reduce annotation effort while preserving accuracy. Implements a two-part prototype system (TILTer annotation UI + TILTify learning/inference service) aligned with the GDPR-oriented TILT machine-readable policy language. Benchmarks three extraction model families under simulated data scarcity for retrieving GDPR data subject rights and finds SentenceBERT (SBERT) with triplet loss provides substantially better top-k retrieval and probability calibration than Binary BERT sequence classification and static-embedding + Gaussian Naive Bayes baselines.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Formulates extraction as paragraph (“blob”) classification per right: estimate P(c_i=1|b_i) and return top-k blobs as suggestions S to annotators; user-confirmed annotations are fed back for continual model updates. Uses three model candidates: (1) spaCy Token2Vec static embeddings + Gaussian Naive Bayes; (2) BinaryBERT: German BERT fine-tuned per right for binary sequence classification with BCE loss and AdamW; (3) SBERT: German SBERT fine-tuned using triplet loss where the anchor/query is the corresponding GDPR legal text for a right, positive examples are blobs containing that right, and negatives are other blobs; margin ε controls separation. Employs weighted sampling to counter class imbalance. Evaluates both retrieval (top-k / k-rank) effectiveness and calibration (weighted Brier score).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Dataset: newly released public corpus of 60 German privacy policies annotated in TILT format; policies segmented into 16,635 paragraph blobs with strong imbalance (only ~77–95 positive blobs per right). Labels: five GDPR data subject rights (information, deletion/correction, portability, withdraw consent, complain). Annotation process: two rounds—initial student annotation followed by senior validation/cleanup. Experimental protocol: fixed train/test split shared across models; 2 repetitions to average over random initialization. Training: GNB 100 epochs; BBERT and SBERT 5 epochs fine-tuning; BBERT uses AdamW (lr 1e-3, weight decay 1e-5); German BERT base (dbmdz/bert-base-german-cased). Metrics: F1 for k-rank retrieval (success if any true positive appears in top-k predictions; k=5/10/25) plus standard classification F1; calibration via weighted Brier score. Main result: SBERT achieves high 5-rank F1 (~0.86–0.93 across rights) and much lower Brier scores (≈0.01–0.05) vs BBERT/GNB which largely fail under scarcity.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Scope limited to paragraph-level retrieval of sentence-like statements (data subject rights) and assumes each policy contains each right (no completeness checking), so method is not evaluated for absence detection. Small dataset (60 docs) in German only; generalization to other languages, jurisdictions, or evolving policy distributions not established. Retrieval is evaluated as ‘at least one correct paragraph in top-k’, which may overestimate utility when multiple relevant passages exist or when precision among top-k matters. Baselines use mostly default hyperparameters and limited repetitions; deeper tuning or stronger legal-domain models could change comparisons. Continuous learning may accumulate annotator errors and bias without robust quality control; prototype architecture not yet production-grade for scale or multi-user concurrency beyond basic design.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Extend extraction beyond paragraph-level to token/span-level facts required by TILT (e.g., controller identity, recipients, transfers) using sequence labeling or hybrid retrieval+NER models; investigate model choice per legal fact granularity. Add completeness/absence detection for rights and other mandated disclosures. Improve MLOps/serving for scalable continuous learning (e.g., Spark/MLflow), versioning, monitoring, and automated regression testing on held-out sets to prevent degradation from noisy annotations. Explore active learning and uncertainty sampling to prioritize annotator effort, plus better calibration techniques. Evaluate cross-lingual transfer, domain adaptation, and robustness to distribution shift across sectors and policy updates; expand datasets and compare with more modern transformer retrievers (bi-encoders/cross-encoders) and legal-domain pretraining.

research_study_list.title: Navigate through Enigmatic Labyrinth A Survey of Chain of Thought Reasoning: Advances, Frontiers and Future
research_study_list.full_text: arXiv:2311.17387v3  [math.AC]  28 Dec 2023 CONDITIONS OF MUL TIPLICITY AND APPLICA TIONS FOR ALMOST GORENSTEIN GRADED RINGS KOJI MATSUSHITA AND SORA MIYASHITA Abstract. In this paper, we prove that if Cohen–Macaulay local/graded rings A, B and R satisfy certain conditions regarding multiplicity and Coh en–Macaulay type, then almost Gorenstein property of R implies Gorenstein properties for all of A, B and R. We apply our theorem to tensor products of semi-standard grade d rings and some classes of aﬃne semigroup rings, i.e., numerical semigroup rings, edg e rings and stable set rings. 1. Introduction An almost Gorenstein ring, which we focus on in this paper, is one of generalized notion of Gorenstein rings. This notion was initially introduced b y Barucci and Fr¨ oberg [1] in the case where the local rings are of dimension one and analytica lly unramiﬁed. Their work inspired Goto, Matsuoka and Phuong [7] to extend the notion o f almost Gorenstein local rings for any one-dimensional Cohen–Macaulay rings. Moreo ver, Goto, Takahashi and Taniguchi [8] deﬁned the almost Gorenstein local rings for a ny dimension. After that, the h-vectors of almost Gorenstein standard graded rings are stu died by Higashitani in [16]. The almost Gorenstein property of aﬃne semigroup rings such as numerical semigroups [14, 21], edge rings [2, 17, 18] and stable set rings [20] has b een studied. Let (R, m) be a Cohen–Macaulay local ring. Then R is said to be an almost Gorenstein local ring if R admits a canonical module ω R and there exists an R-monomorphism φ :֒→ω R such that C := coker(φ) is an Ulrich R-module, i.e., µ (C) = e(C) ([8, Deﬁnition 3.3]). Here, µ (C) (resp. e(C)) denotes the number of elements in a minimal generating sys tem of C (resp. the multiplicity of C with respect to m). The zero module is regarded as an Ulrich R-module. Similarly to local rings, a Cohen–Macaulay graded ring R = ⨁ n≥ 0Rn with k = R0 a ﬁeld is called an almost Gorenstein graded ring if R admits a graded canonical module ω R and there exists an R-monomorphism φ :֒→ω R(−aR) of degree 0 such that C := coker(φ) is an Ulrich R-module ([8, Deﬁnition 8.1]). Here, aR = − min{i : (ω R)i ̸= 0 } is the a-invariant of R and ω R(−aR) is the graded R-module deﬁned as having the same underlying R-module structure as ω R with the grading determined by [ω R(−aR)]n = [ω R]n− aR for all n ∈ Z. In this paper, we introduce the following. Deﬁnition 1.1. Let (R, m) be a Cohen–Macaulay local ring that admits a canonical module ω R. Assume that there exists an R-monomorphism φ :֒→ω R and we deﬁne 2020 Mathematics Subject Classiﬁcation. Primary 13H10; Secondary 13M05. Key words and phrases. almost Gorenstein, multiplicity, tensor products, edge ri ngs, stable set rings, numerical semigroup rings. 1δφ(R) as e(coker(φ)). When there is no risk of confusion about the monomorphism we simply write δφ(R) as δ(R) without explicitly mentioning φ. Moteover, we call R is almost Gorenstein with respect to φ if coker(φ) is an Ulrich R-module. We call R satisﬁes (∗) if there exists an R-monomorphism φ :֒→ω R such that φ(1R) /∈ mω R. If R is non-regular, then condition ( ∗) is a necessary condition for R to be almost Gorenstein (see [8, Corollary 3.10]). We introduce the grad ed versions of above. Deﬁnition 1.2. LetR = ⨁ i≥ 0Ri be a Cohen–Macaulay graded ring. We call R satisﬁes (∗) if there exists an R-monomorphism φ :֒→ω R(−aR) of degree 0. Note that φ satisﬁes φ(1R) /∈ mω R where m = ⨁ n>0Rn. If R satisﬁes ( ∗), take an R-monomorphism φ :֒→ω R(−aR) of degree 0 and we deﬁne δφ(R) as e(coker(φ)). When there is no risk of confusion about the monomorphism we simply write δφ(R) as δ(R). Moreover, we call R is almost Gorenstein with respect to φ if coker(φ) is an Ulrich R-module. Now let us explain our main theorem. Let A and B be positively graded k-algebras and let R be a k-algebra constructed from A and B in a certain way. In this case, some ring-theoretic properties of R may be inherited by A andB. For example, let R =A ⊗ kB be the tensor product of A and B, then it is known that A and B are Gorenstein if and only if R is Gorenstein. Actually, a similar assertion holds for the t ensor product of almost Gorenstein semi-standard graded rings and the “gluing” of n umerical semigroups deﬁned by [25, 26], which is a distinct operation from tensor produc t. In this paper, a more general statement is proved that extend s the cases of the ten- sor product of semi-standard graded rings and the gluing of n umerical semigroups. The following are our main results: Here, r(R) denotes the Cohen–Macaulay type of R. Theorem 1.3 (see Theorem 3.2). LetA,B andR be Cohen–Macaulay local rings. Assume that A and B satisfy (∗) and there exists an R-monomorphism φ :֒→ω R. Moreover, we assume e(A)> 1, e(B)> 1 and δφ(R) ≥ e(B)δ(A) +e(A)δ(B). (1.1) Then the following conditions are equivalent: (1) R is almost Gorenstein with respect to φ and r(R) ≤ r(A)r(B); (2) A and B Gorenstein and the equality of (1.1) holds. If this is the case, R is Gorenstein. If we consider graded case, we have the following. Theorem 1.4 (see Theorem 3.3) . LetA, B and R be Cohen–Macaulay positively graded rings. Assume that A, B and R satisfy (∗), e(A)> 1, e(B)> 1 and δφ(R) ≥ e(B)δ(A) +e(A)δ(B). (1.2) Then the following conditions are equivalent: (1) R is almost Gorenstein with respect to φ and r(R) ≤ r(A)r(B); (2) R is Gorenstein; (3) A and B Gorenstein and the equality of (1.2) holds. When does the inequality (1.2) hold? Actually, the tensor pr oduct of semi-standard graded rings or the gluing of numerical semigroups satisﬁes the inequality (1.2). So we get the following results about the tensor products (or the quot ient rings divided by its regular sequences) and the gluing of numerical semigroups. The seco nd one is a well-known result (see [21, Theorem 6.7] and [25]). 2Corollary 1.5 (see Corollary 3.10). LetA andB be semi-standard graded Cohen–Macaulay k-algebras over a ﬁeld k such that A and B satisfy (∗) and e(A)> 1, e(B)> 1. In addi- tion, let T =A ⊗ kB and let x =x1,...,x n be a homogeneous regular sequence on T with deg(xk) = ak for 1 ≤ k ≤ n such that T/ (x) satisﬁes (∗). Set R =T or R =T/ (x), then the following conditions are equivalent: (1) R is almost Gorenstein; (2) R is Gorenstein; (3) A and B are Gorenstein. Corollary 1.6 (see Corollary 4.2) . LetH1 and H2 be numerical semigroups and let H be the gluing of H1 and H2. Then the following conditions are equivalent: (1) k[H] is almost Gorenstein; (2) k[H] is Gorenstein; (3) k[H1] and k[H2] are Gorenstein. Here, k[H] denotes the numerical semigroup ring of H. Please refer to subsection 2.2 for the deﬁnition of the gluing of numerical semigroups. We also provide applications of our results to two toric ring s arising from graphs; edge rings and stable set rings. Theorem 1.7 (see Theorem 4.5) . Let G1 and G2 be simple graphs. Suppose that G1 is bipartite and k[G2] is Cohen–Macaulay with e(k[Gi])> 1 for i = 1, 2. Then the following are equivalent: (1) k[G1♯G2] is almost Gorenstein; (2) k[G1♯G2] is Gorenstein; (3) k[G1] and k[G2] are Gorenstein. Here, k[G] denotes the edge ring of a graph G and G1♯G2 denotes the clique sum of G1 and G2. Theorem 1.8 (see Theorem 4.6). LetG1 andG2 be simple graphs. Suppose that Stabk(Gi) is Cohen–Macaulay with e(Stabk(Gi))> 1 for i = 1, 2. Then the following are equivalent: (1) Stab k(G1 +G2) is almost Gorenstein; (2) Stab k(G1 +G2) is Gorenstein; (3) Stab k(G1) and Stabk(G2) are Gorenstein. Here, Stab k(G) denotes the stable set ring of a graph G and G1 +G2 denotes the join of G1 and G2. The structure of this paper is as follows. In Section 2, we pre pare some facts and deﬁni- tions for the discussions later; we collect basic deﬁnition s and properties of commutative rings, numerical semigroups and toric rings arising from gr aphs. In Section 3, we show our main theorem. We also discuss conditions appearing in our ma in theorem. Moreover, we apply our theorem to tensor products of semi-standard grade d rings. In Section 4, we ap- ply our main theorem to some classes of aﬃne semigroup rings, i.e., numerical semigroup rings, edge rings and stable set rings. Acknowledgement. We are grateful to Professor Akihiro Higashitani for his com ments and instructive discussions. The ﬁrst author is partially s upported by Grant-in-Aid for JSPS Fellows Grant JP22J20033. 32. Preliminaries The goal of this section is to prepare the required materials for the discussions of our main results. 2.1. Preliminaries on commutative rings. In this subsection, we recall some funda- mental materials (consult, e.g., [3] for the introduction t o Cohen–Macaulay rings). • Let ω R denote a canonical module of R. Let aR denote the a-invariant of R, i.e., aR = − min{n : (ω R)n ̸= 0}. • For an R-module M , we use the following notation: – Let µ (M ) denote the number of a minimal generating system of M as an R-module. – Let e(M ) denote the multiplicity of M . – Note that the inequality µ (M ) ≤ e(M ) always holds. • Letr(R) denote the Cohen–Macaulay type of R. Note that r(R) = µ (ω R). We see that Cohen–Macaulay ring R is Gorenstein if and only if r(R) = 1. • For a graded R-module M , we denote H( M,t ) the Hilbert series of M , i.e., H(M,t ) = ∑ n∈ Z dimkMntn, where dim k stands for the dimension as a k-vector space. • LetR = ⨁ i≥ 0Ri be a graded ring. R is called semi-standard graded ifR is ﬁnitely generated k[R1]-module. Moreover, R is called standard graded if R = k[R1]. If R is a semi-standard graded ring, its Hilbert series is of the f orm H(R,t ) = h0 +h1t + · · ·+hsts (1 − t)dim R where h0 +h1t + · · ·+hsts ∈ Z[t]. We call this polynomial the h-polynomial of R and denote it as hR(t). Note that ∑s i=0hi ̸= 0 and hs ̸= 0. We call the integer sequence ( h0,h 1,...,h s) the h-vector of R. We always have h0 = 1. If a semi-standard graded ring R is Cohen–Macaulay, its h-vector satisﬁes hi ≥ 0 for any i. Moreover, if R is standard graded, we have hi > 0 for any i. For further information on the h-vectors of Cohen–Macaulay semi-standard (resp. standard ) graded rings, see [28] (resp. [29]). Note that hs ≤ r(R) holds. Moreover, we see that d +aR =s and e(R) = h0 +h1 + · · ·+hs (see [3, Section 4.4]). LetR be a local (resp. positively graded) Cohen–Macaulay ring. A ssume thatR satisﬁes (∗) and take a (graded) monomorphism φ :֒→ω R with 1 R /∈ mω R. Put C = coker(φ). ThenC is a Cohen–Macaulay R-module of dimension d − 1 if C ̸= 0 (see [8, Lemma 3.1]). Note that C = 0 if and only if R is Gorenstein. Moreover, there are some relation between µ (C) and r(R). We can check the following by the same proof as [16, Proposi tion 2.2]. Proposition 2.1. LetR be a Cohen–Macaulay local (resp. positively graded) ring whi ch satisﬁes (∗). Then we have µ (C) = r(R) − 1. Let R be a semi-standard graded ring, then the following result is true. This is a semi-standard graded version of [16, Proposition 2.4]. Proposition 2.2 (see [19, Theorem 4.5]) . Let R be a Cohen–Macaulay semi-standard graded ring which satisﬁes (∗) and let (h0,...,h s) be itsh-vector. Take any monomorphism 4φ :֒→ω R(−aR) with degree 0. Then the Hilbert series of C := coker(φ) is H(C,t ) = ∑s− 1 j=0((hs + · · ·+hs− j) − (h0 + · · ·+hj))tj (1 − t)dim R− 1 . (2.1) In particular, δφ(R) = ∑s− 1 j=0((hs + · · ·+hs− j) − (h0 + · · ·+hj)) = ∑s j=0(2j − s)hj. The following is well-known, but we also provide a proof. Proposition 2.3. LetR be a Cohen–Macaulay local (or positively graded) ring. If e(R) ≥ 2, then we have r(R)<e (R). Proof. Put d = dimR. Since R is Cohen–Macaulay, there exists a regular sequence x on m such that e(R) = l(R/ x). Thus we may assume d = 0 since r(R) = r(R/ (x)). By the assumption, we have Soc( R) ̸= R so Soc(R) ⊂ m, where Soc( R) = {x ∈ R : x m = 0 }. Therefore, we have e(R) = dim k Soc(R) ≤ dimk m< dimkR =e(R) so r(R)<e (R). □ The following is a formula for calculating the Hilbert serie s of the canonical module. Proposition 2.4 ([3, Corollary 4.4.6 (a)]) . Let k be a ﬁeld and let R be a d-dimensional Cohen–Macaulay positively graded k-algebra. Then H(ω R(−aR),t ) = ( − 1)dtaR H(R,t − 1). 2.2. Numerical semigroups. In this subsection, we recall the deﬁnitions and properties related to numerical semigroups. See, e.g., [27] for the int roduction to numerical semi- group. A numerical semigroup H is a subset of N which is closed under addition, contains the zero element and whose complement in N is ﬁnite. We denote k[H] as the numerical semigroup ring of H Every numerical semigroup H admits a ﬁnite generating system, that is, there exist a1,...,a n ∈ H withH = ⟨a1,...,a n⟩ = {λ 1a1 + · · ·+λ nan |λ 1,...,λ n ∈ N}. Let H be a numerical semigroup. We denote G(H) = {a1 < a2 < · · ·< an} as the minimal generating system of H. We call a1 the multiplicity of H and denote it by mult(H), and we call n the embedding dimension of H and denote it by emb( H). In general, emb(H) ≤ mult(H). The ﬁnite set N \H is called the set of gaps of H. If H is a numerical semigroup, the largest integer in N \H is called Frobenius number of H and we denote it by F( H). We say that H is symmetric if for every z ∈ Z, either z ∈ H or F(H) − z ∈ H. We say that an integer x is a pseudo-Frobenius number of H ifx ̸∈ H and x +h ∈ H for all h ∈ H,h ̸= 0. We denote the set of pseudo-Frobenius numbers of H by PF(H). The cardinality in PF( H) is called the type of H, denoted by t( H). It is known that mult(H) = e(k[H]), t(H) = r(k[H]) (see [9]). Moreover, H is symmetric if and only if k[H] is Gorenstein (see [11]). Let H be a numerical semigroup and let s ∈ H \ {0}; the Ap´ eryset with respect to a nonzero s ∈ H is deﬁned as Ap(H,s ) = {a ∈ H :a − s /∈ H}. Note that |Ap(H,s )|=s and there is a relation between Ap´ ery sets and Hilbert serie s. Proposition 2.5 (see [24]) . LetH be a numerical semigroup and let s ∈ H \ {0}; H(k[H],t ) = 1 1 − ts ∑ a∈ Ap(H,s) ta The concept of the gluing of numerical semigroups was deﬁned in [25, 26]. Deﬁnition 2.6 (see [25, 26]) . Let H1 = ⟨a1,a 2,...,a n⟩ and H2 = ⟨b1,b 2,...,b m⟩ be numerical semigroups. Take y ∈ H1 \G(H1) and x ∈ H2 \G(H2) such that gcd( x,y ) = 1. We call H = ⟨xH1,yH 2⟩ = ⟨xa1,xa 2,...,xa n,yb 1,yb 2,...,yb m⟩ 5the gluing of H1 and H2 (with respect to x and y). The following is well-known result about the gluing of numer ical semigroups. Theorem 2.7 ([21, Theorem 6.3] and [25]) . LetH1 and H2 be numerical semigroups and let H be the gluing of H1 and H2. Then the following are true: (1) If k[H1] and k[H2] are Gorenstein, then k[H] is Gorenstein; (2) If k[H] is almost Gorenstein, then k[H1] and k[H2] are Gorenstein. Now, let H = ⟨xH1,yH 2⟩ be the gluing of H1 and H2 with gcd(x,y ) = 1 and xy ∈ xH1 ∩ yH2. Nari ([21]) proved the following to show Theorem 2.7 (2). Lemma 2.8 (see [21, Lemma 6.5 and Proposition 6.6]) . LetH = ⟨xH1,yH 2⟩ be the gluing of H1 and H2, then we have Ap(H,xy ) = {xs +yt |s ∈ Ap(H1,y ),t ∈ Ap(H2,x )}, PF(H) = {xf +yf ′ +xy |f ∈ PF(H1),f ′ ∈ PF(H2)} with t(H) = t(H1) t(H2). In particular, F (H) = x F(H1) +y F(H2) +xy. 2.3. T oric rings arising from graphs. In this subsection, we prepare some notions and notation on graphs and deﬁne edge rings and stable set rings, which are toric rings arising from graphs. For the fundamental materials on graph theory a nd toric rings, consult, e.g., [5] and [13], respectively. For a positive integer d, let [ d] := {1,...,d }. Consider a ﬁnite simple graph G on the vertex set V (G) = [d] with the edge set E(G). We say that S ⊂ V (G) is a stable set or an independent set (resp. a clique) if {v,w } ̸∈ E(G) (resp. {v,w } ∈ E(G)) for any distinct verticesv,w ∈ S. Note that the empty set and each singleton are regarded as st able sets. We introduce two ways to construct a new graph from two given g raphs G1 and G2: • Suppose that V (G1) ∩ V (G2) is a clique of both G1 and G2. Then the clique sum G1♯G2 of G1 and G2 along V (G1) ∩ V (G2) is the graph on the vertex set V (G1) ∪ V (G2) with the edge set E(G1) ∪ E(G2). • Suppose that V (G1) ∩ V (G2) = ∅. Then the join G1 +G2 of G1 and G2 is the graph on the vertex set V (G1) ∪V (G2) with the edge set E(G1) ∪E(G2) ∪ {{i,j } : i ∈ V (G1),j ∈ V (G2)}. Now, we deﬁne our toric rings: Deﬁnition 2.9. Consider the morphism φ G of k-algebras: φ G : k[x{i,j} : {i,j } ∈ E(G)] → k[t1,...,t d], induced by φ G(x{i,j}) = titj. Then we denote the image (resp. the kernel) of φ G by k[G] (resp. IG) and call k[G] the edge ring of G. Deﬁnition 2.10. Consider the morphism ψ G of k-algebras: ψ G : k[xS :S is a stable set of G] → k[t1,...,t d+1], induced by ψ G(xS) = ( ∏ i∈ S ti ) td+1. Then we denote the image (resp. the kernel) of ψ G by Stab k(G) (resp. JG) and call Stabk(G) the stable set ring of G. The edge ring (resp. stable set ring) of G is regarded as a standard graded k-algebra by setting deg(φ G(x{i,j})) = 1 (resp. deg( ψ G(xS)) = 1). Moreover, we can regard IGi (resp. JGi) as a subset of IG (resp. JG) since each edge (resp. stable set) of G1 or G2 is that of G1♯G2 (resp. G1 +G2). 63. Conditions for almost Gorenstein rings In this section, we show our main theorem and discuss conditi ons appearing in our main theorem. Moreover, we apply our theorem to tensor produ cts of semi-standard graded rings and the quotient rings divided by its regular se quences. 3.1. Proof of main theorem. The following is a key lemma. Lemma 3.1. LetA and B be Cohen–Macaulay local (or graded) rings with e(A)> 1 and e(B)> 1. If there exist γ1,γ 2 ∈ R≥ 0 such that r(A) ≤ γ1 + 1, r(B) ≤ γ2 + 1 and e(B)γ1 +e(A)γ2 ≤ r(A)r(B) − 1, (3.1) then A and B are Gorenstein and γi = 0 for i = 1, 2. Proof. We get the following inequality by the assumptions: e(B)γ1 +e(A)γ2 ≤ r(A)r(B) − 1 ≤ γ1γ2 +γ1 +γ2. (3.2) Thus we have X := (e(A) − 1 − γ1)(e(B) − 1 − γ2) − (e(A) − 1)(e(B) − 1) ≥ 0. It follows from (e(A) − 1)(e(B) − 1) > 0 and X ≥ 0 that (e(A) − 1 − γ1)(e(B) − 1 − γ2)> 0, equivalently, “e(A) − 1 − γ1 > 0 and e(B) − 1 − γ2 > 0” or “ e(A) − 1 − γ1 < 0 and e(B) − 1 − γ2 < 0”. Suppose that e(A) − 1 − γ1 < 0 and e(B) − 1 − γ2 < 0. This hypothesis and Proposition 2.3 imply that e(B)γ1 +e(A)γ2 >e (B)(e(A) − 1) +e(A)(e(B) − 1) ≥ r(B)(r(A) − 1) +r(A)(r(B) − 1) ≥ r(A)r(B) − 1, a contradiction to (3.1). Application to edge rings and stable set rings. In this subsection, we prove Theorems 1.7 and 1.8. Before that, we discuss toric splittin gs of toric ideals, which helps us to show our results. Toric ideals are prime ideals of polynomial rings generated by binomials . We say that a toric ideal I of a polynomial ring S over k has toric splitting (or I is a splittable toric ideal) if there exist toric ideals I1 and I2 ofS such that I =I1 +I2. See [6] for details on toric splittings. For a polynomial f of S, we denote the set of variables of S appearing in f by var(f ) and for a subset F of S, we deﬁne var(F ) := ⋃ f ∈ F var(f ). Proposition 4.3. LetI be a toric ideal of S generated by binomials f1,...,f n,g 1,...,g m and let B1 = {f1,...,f n} and B2 = {g1,...,g m}. Suppose that I has the toric splitting I = (B1) + (B2), that var(B1 ∪ B2) contains all variables of S and that var(B1) ∩ var(B2) consists a single variable z. Then we have S/I ∼ = ( k[var( ˜B1)]/ ( ˜B1) ⊗ k k[var( ˜B2)]/ ( ˜B2) ) / (z1 − z2), wherez1 and z2 are new variables that do not belong to S and we let ˜fi (resp. ˜gi) be the polynomial obtained by substituting z1 (resp. z2) for z appearing in fi (resp. gi) and let ˜B1 = { ˜f1,..., ˜fn} and ˜B2 = {˜g1,..., ˜gm}. Proof. Since var(B1 ∪ B2) contains all variables of S and I has the toric splitting I = (B1) + (B2), we have k[var(B1 ∪ B2)]/ ((B1) + (B2)) = S/I . Moreover, it follows from var( ˜B1) ∩ var( ˜B2) = ∅ that k[var( ˜B1)]/ ( ˜B1) ⊗ k k[var( ˜B2)]/ ( ˜B2) ∼ = k[var( ˜B1 ∪ ˜B2)]/ (( ˜B1) + ( ˜B2)), and hence S/I = k[var(B1 ∪ B2)]/ ((B1) + (B2)) ∼ = k[var( ˜B1 ∪ ˜B2)]/ (( ˜B1) + ( ˜B2) + (z1 − z2)) ∼ = ( k[var( ˜B1)]/ ( ˜B1) ⊗ k k[var( ˜B2)]/ ( ˜B2) ) / (z1 − z2). □ Corollary 4.4. Work with the notation and hypothesis of Proposition 4.3, an d assume that A := k[var( ˜B1)]/ ( ˜B1), B := k[var( ˜B2)]/ ( ˜B2) andR :=S/I are semi-standard graded. Then the following conditions are equivalent: (1) R is almost Gorenstein; (2) R is Gorenstein; (3) A and B are Gorenstein. Proof. It follows from Corollary 3.10 and Proposition 4.3 (even if v ar(B1 ∪ B2) does not necessarily contain all variables of S, we can show this claim by applying polynomial extension and Corollary 3.11). □ 11We now give the proofs of our theorems and some examples. Firs t, we provide an application to edge rings: Theorem 4.5. Let G1 and G2 be simple graphs. Suppose that G1 is bipartite and k[G2] is Cohen–Macaulay with e(k[Gi])> 1 for i = 1, 2. Then the following are equivalent: (1) k[G1♯G2] is almost Gorenstein; (2) k[G1♯G2] is Gorenstein; (3) k[G1] and k[G2] are Gorenstein. Proof. It is known that the edge ring of a bipartite graph is normal ([ 22, Corollary 2.3]), and hence k[G1] is Cohen–Macaulay. Note that k := |V (G1) ∩ V (G2)|≤ 2 since G1 is bipartite. If k ≤ 1, then we have k[G1♯G2] ∼ = k[G1] ⊗ k k[G2] (cf. [30, Proposition 10.1.48]). Therefore, conditions (1), (2) and (3) are equivalent by Cor ollary 3.10. Suppose that k = 2 and let c =V (G1) ∩ V (G2) (note that c ∈ E(G)). We can see that IG has the toric splitting IG = IG1 +IG2 ([6, Corollary 4.8]) and the common variable that appears in generators of IG1 and IG2 is only xc. Therefore, we get the desired result from Corollary 4.4. □ Next, we present an application to stable set rings: Theorem 4.6. Let G1 and G2 be simple graphs. Suppose that Stabk(Gi) is Cohen– Macaulay with e(Stabk(Gi))> 1 for i = 1, 2. It follows immediately from Corollary 4.4 and Lemma 4.7 belo w. □ Lemma 4.7. (a) For two integers m ≥ 1 and l ≥ 0, let gl r1,...,rm be the graph consisting of rj cycles of length 2 j + 1 and l even cycles, such that all cycles share a single common vertex. If l = 0, then gl r1,...,rm consists of only odd cycles and we denote it by gr1,...,rm. This graph is the clique sum of gr1,...,rm andl even cycles. It is known that the edge ring of an even cycle C is Gorenstein with e(k[C])> 1 (cf. [23, Theorem 2.1(b)]) and that almost Gorensteinness of the edge ring of gr1,...,rm has been investigated in [2, Theorem 1.2]. From these facts and Theorem 4.5, we can characterize when k[gl r1,...,rm] is (almost) Gorenstein: • k[gl r1,...,rm] is Gorenstein if and only if r1 + · · ·+rm ≤ 2. • k[gl r1,...,rm] is not Gorenstein but alomst Gorenstein if and only if l = 0, m = 1 and r1 ≥ 3. (b) Let G1,...,G k be simple connected graphs with at most one Gi not being bipartite and let C be an even cycle with at least k edges. In addition, let G be the graph obtained by identifying an edge of Gi with a distinct edge of C for each i (this graph appears in [6, Theorem 3.7]). If e(k[Gi])> 1 for all i, then we have the following equivalent conditions: k[G] is almost Gorenstein ⇔ k[G] is Gorenstein ⇔ k[Gi] is Gorenstein for all i. Example 4.9. Form ≥ 3 and n ≥ 1, we consider the cone graph Cm + K n, which is the join of the cycle graph Cm of length m and the empty graph K n of order n. It is known that • the stable set ring of Cm is almost Gorenstein ([20, Theorem 4.1]). In particular, it is Gorenstein if and only if m is even, m = 3 or 5 ([23, Theorem 2.1(b)] and [15, Theorem 1]). • the stable set ring of K n is isomorphic to the Segre product of n polynomial rings in 2 variables and is Gorenstein. Note that e(Stabk(K n)) = 1 if and only if n = 1. Therefore, it follows from Theorem 4.6 that • Stabk(Cm +Kn) is Gorenstein if and only if m is even, m = 3 or 5. • Stabk(Cm +K n) is not Gorenstein but almost Gorenstein if and only if n = 1, m is odd and m ≥ 7.
